title: Colocation of skill related suppliers -- Revisiting coagglomeration using firm-to-firm network data
abstract: Aggregated data on labor flows and input-output connections in Hungary (2015-2017). Related preprint: https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.07071 Interactive visualization site: https://vis.csh.ac.at/colocation-suppliers/ The related research by Sándor Juhász was supported by the European Union’s Marie Sklodowska-Curie Postdoctoral Fellowship Program (SUPPED, grant number 101062606). The data preparation was done with the help of the Databank of HUN-REN Centre for Economic and Regional Studies. The shared datasets are based on the value-added tax return data files of the Hungarian Central Statistical Office. The calculations and conclusions drawn from them are the sole intellectual property of the authors.
summary_en: Classic economic-geography theory says firms cluster near other firms they share skilled workers with (labour pooling) or trade inputs with (input-output linkages). This paper tests the theory with fine-grained Hungarian data: actual firm-to-firm labour flows and actual firm-to-firm transactions, 2015–2017. Firms that share workers or trade directly are much more likely to be geographically co-located than firms sharing only industry codes. The result validates the Marshallian externalities theory but shows that traditional aggregated tests (using industry-level SIC/NAICS codes) miss most of the action.
data_used: Hungarian firm-level administrative data 2015–2017: labour flows (matched employer-employee panel) and firm-to-firm transactions (VAT return data) from KRTK Databank; all incorporated firms.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian regional development and industrial policy (HIPA, Ministry of Economy, regional agencies): supports place-based policies that target specific firm clusters identified through actual linkages, not just industry codes. Relevant when deciding where to locate new industrial parks or training-programme facilities — labour-pool and supplier-network proximity beats generic industry classifications. Hungary-specific; findings directly operational.
